narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and enrollment plans, focusing on patterns seen in past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 class rank, which fell in the second decile at your school—much lower than the typical fifth or sixth decile among students who went on to pass the bar. Next in importance was your LSAT score of 35.5, which, while solid, pulled somewhat in your favor compared to passers' average of 37.27, helping to offset the risk slightly. Your Year 1 class rank in the third decile also contributed toward the failure prediction, as it was notably below the sixth decile average for successful bar takers. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket—which aligns with about a third of passers—and your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, close to the 3.24 average, played smaller roles, as did your full-time enrollment plans that match most admitted students. Overall, your ranks in those early and later undergrad years stood out as the biggest drags compared to profiles of accepted applicants, who typically show stronger consistent performance across the board. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven signal based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your academic standing through additional coursework or a post-bac program before reapplying. You might also seek mentorship from law school alumni who succeeded with similar starting points, or explore part-time programs if that fits your circumstances better. With targeted improvements, especially in demonstrating upward academic trends, you can shift this prediction in your favor for future applications. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these key areas head-on.
